coredata.dc:title: Luminescence properties of silk cocoon derived carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles/PVA hybrid film

Zheng, Mingtao
Dong, Hanwu
Lei, Bingfu
Zhang, Haoran
Xiao, Yong
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: Carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) with broad particle distribution were synthesized by thermal-treatment using carbonation of silk cocoon as raw materials. The experimental results showed tunable emission wavelength of the nanoparticles. The quantum yield of the CFNs was calculated to be 23%. A CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film was prepared from a blend of the CFNs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) showing interesting green afterglow which could be observed by naked eyes. The afterglow spectrum revealed that the CFNs/PVA thin film had a broad afterglow emission peak located at 520nm. The afterglow intensity decay curve of the film showed visually recognizable period longer than 140s. The PVA–PVA hydrogen bonding in the PVA matrix may play a key role for the afterglow.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles
Polyvinyl alcohol
Afterglow

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CFNs from silk cocoon
Preparation of CFNs/PVA composites thin film
Photoluminescence properties of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21171071 , 21371062 , 51372091 ), the project of the combination of Industry and Research by the Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2012B091100181 ), the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842 ), and the Science and Technology Innovating Project funded by the Guangdong Provincial Educational Commission of China (Grant No. 2013KJCX0030 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of the CFNs and (b) TEM image of the CFNs. The inset histogram in (b) is size distribution of the CFNs measured by TEM.
FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black), photoluminescence emission spectrum (blue) and photoluminescence excitation spectrum (red) of a diluted aqueous solution of the CFNs (inset is the digital photographs of the CFNs aqueous solution under daylight and UV light) and (b) photoluminescence emission spectra of a diluted aqueous solution of the CFNs under different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film at various excitation wavelengths and (b) photoluminescence emission spectra of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film, the CFNs/PVA solution and the CFNs aqueous solution at 360nm excitation.
Afterglow intensity decay curve of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film. Inset: digital photographs of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film under daylight (left), and after removal of UV irradiation (right).
Afterglow emission spectrum under 305nm excitation and phosphorescence excitation spectrum monitored at 480nm of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film.

head.ce:title: Luminescence properties of silk cocoon derived carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles/PVA hybrid film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) with broad particle distribution were synthesized by thermal-treatment using carbonation of silk cocoon as raw materials. The experimental results showed tunable emission wavelength of the nanoparticles. The quantum yield of the CFNs was calculated to be 23%. A CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film was prepared from a blend of the CFNs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) showing interesting green afterglow which could be observed by naked eyes. The afterglow spectrum revealed that the CFNs/PVA thin film had a broad afterglow emission peak located at 520nm. The afterglow intensity decay curve of the film showed visually recognizable period longer than 140s. The PVA–PVA hydrogen bonding in the PVA matrix may play a key role for the afterglow.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of a new class of carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) derived from the single-walled carbon nanotube by arc-discharge methods in 2004 [1], the CFNs have attracted tremendous attention due to their unique properties and potential applications, including fluorescent carbon inks [2], elective detection of metal ions [3], and biological labeling [4], etc. Compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, the CFNs are superior in tunable emission [5], low cytotoxicity [6] and high biocompatibility [7]. Thus far, there are two types of synthetic strategies for preparing CFNs. Firstly, the so called top-down strategy summarizes different methods including chemical oxidation [8], hydrothermal [9], arc-discharge [1] and laser ablation [10], where the carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles are prepared by smashing a larger crystalline carbonaceous structure into small pieces. In an opposite direction, the bottom–up approaches prepare CFNs by combustion [11], thermal decomposition [12], and dehydration of suitable carbonaceous precursors [13]. However, the above mentioned methods involve intricate processes, expensive starting materials or rigorous experimental conditions which hindered their applications.

Recently, there has been a trend to prepare CFNs using biomass materials as starting materials, such as plant leaf [14], willow bark [15], orange juice [16], and egg [2], due to their low cost, easy acquisition, and nontoxicity. Among the various biomass candidates, silk cocoon, a nontoxic protein-based biopolymer which is composed of fibrous proteins and sericin and has a good biocompatibility, is promising for the preparation of CFNs. Compared with other biomass materials, silk cocoon has much higher nitrogen content (ca. 18.36–18.65%) due to it abundant proteins content [17], which makes it a significant precursor for N-doped CFNs. Li et al. [18] obtained N-doped photoluminescent carbonaceous nanospheres with an average diameter of ca. 70nm by hydrothermal treatment of cocoon silk at 200°C for 72h. Wu et al. [17] prepared 4–7nm N-doped amphoteric carbon dots from silk in NaOH aqueous solution by hydrothermal treatment at 190°C for 3h of which a photoluminescence quantum yield of 13.9% were determined. The as-prepared carbon dots are highly photostable, low toxic and suitable for bioimaging. However hydrothermal treatment requires high pressure, sealed condition, and other strict requirements for the equipment.

Herein, we present an easy and low-cost approach for synthesizing CFNs from silk cocoon through a pyrolysis process. Interestingly, when the silk cocoon based CFNs was incorporated into the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, long-lasting afterglow can be seen from the composite film by naked eyes at room temperature after excitation by ultraviolet (UV) light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CFNs from silk cocoon
ce:section.ce:para: The silk cocoon was firstly washed with distilled water and dried at 60°C. Then 0.8g cut-in-piece silk cocoon was heat treated in a tube furnace at 300°C for 4h in a nitrogen (N2, 99.999%) atmosphere with a heating rate of 10°C/min. The resulting pyrolyzed black solids were cooled down to room temperature in the furnace naturally, collected and then grounded in an agate mortar into fine powder. The powder was ultrasonic dispersed in distilled water (the ratio of solid to liquid is 1g:400ml) for 30min, and filtered with a 0.22μm filtration membrane. The filtered solution was further centrifuged at 8000rpm for 30min to obtain a supernatant which contains CFNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CFNs/PVA composites thin film
ce:section.ce:para: 1g PVA was firstly added into 10ml distilled water and then mixed with 1ml of the above-mentioned supernatant under magnetic stirring at 85°C until the PVA was dissolved completely forming a uniform CFNs/PVA mixture. Then the mixture was poured onto a flat glassplate and then dried at 60°C forming a thin film. The thin film was peeled off carefully and then annealed in an oven at 100°C for 30min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a TECNAI 10 (PHILIPS) electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40kV, 20mA, λ =1.54051Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by an Avatar 360 FT-IR (Thermo Nicolet) spectrometer using the KBr pellet technique ranging from 1000 to 4000cm−1. The fluorescence spectra of the CFNs and the CFNs/PVA thin film were measured with Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CFNs aqueous solution was measured with Shimadzu UV-2550. The afterglow decay curve was obtained at room temperature with PR-305 after the sample was exposed to an irradiation from a 1000lx standard xenon lamp for 15min.

The XRD pattern of the CFNs is presented in Fig. 1 a. Except for the broad diffusive peak at about 25°, no sharp peaks can be observed in the XRD pattern which confirms the amorphous character of the CFNs. The typical TEM image (Fig. 1b) shows that the CFNs are mostly of spherical shape. Size distribution of the CFNs was analyzed by measuring diameters of about 100 CFNs in TEM images, which is shown in the inset of Fig. 1b indicating a centered diameter of the CFNs is about 7nm.

Elemental analysis revealed that the CFNs are mainly composed of C (57%), H (3%), and N (15%), The content of C and H is approximately equal to that of most reported CFNs [4,14,18]. The FTIR spectrum of the CFNs as shown in Fig. 2 shows a characteristic absorption bands of hydroxyl groups located at 3480cm−1, the CH stretching vibrations at 2937 and 2875cm−1, and the CO stretching vibrations at 1666cm−1. The NH bending vibrations at 1596cm−1 and the CH bending vibrations at 1130–1064cm−1 can also be observed. These functional groups are produced from the degradation of a wide range of amino acids from silk cocoon, which can improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the CFNs in an aqueous system.

The quantum yield (Q) of the CFNs was calculated by comparative method using Eq. (1), in which Q is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore with known quantum yield. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 with reported quantum yield of 54% at 360nm was chosen as a standard reference. The obtained results are listed in Table 1 . The quantum yield of CFNs in this work is higher than some reported biomass based CFNs [14,17,19]. The relatively high quantum yield of CFNs may caused by the relatively high N content, which is consistent with other reported results that the CFNs with nitrogen groups can significantly enhance their PL properties [15,19–21].
(1)
Q =
Q
R
I
I
R
A
R
A
n
2
n
R
2

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CFNs is shown in Fig. 3 a. Two weak absorption bands, one located at about 280nm and another much broader band located between 260 and 300nm, which can be attributed to the π–π* transitions of CC conjugated double bonds and the n–π* transition of CO bonds, respectively. These results are consistent with the reported CFNs [18,22]. Under 365nm excitation, a broad emission band between 380 and 750nm centering at ca. 435nm can be observed in the emission spectrum (Fig. 3a). When monitoring the 435nm emission, an abroad excitation band ranging from 218nm to 420nm (centering at 365nm) can be observed in the excitation spectrum which is also shown in Fig. 3a. Under 365nm UV excitation, there is strong blue photoluminescence (PL) from the dilute CFNs aqueous solution (see inset of Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b shows the excitation-dependent emission spectra of the CFNs. It is obvious that the photoluminescence peak correspondingly shifts from 400nm to 480nm while the excitation wavelength changes from 300nm to 420nm. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of the CFNs prepared from silk cocoon is similar to the other reported CFNs [7,23].

It should be noted that the PL mechanisms of CFNs are very complicated and still argumentative. However, more and more studies attributed the CFNs’ PL to differently sized nanoparticles (quantum effect) and surface-defects existed in the CFNs. Dong et al. [24] considered the doping of nitrogen can introduce the CFNs a new kind of surface state. Electrons trapped by the new formed surface states are able to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination. And the different kinds of surface states corresponding to a relatively wide distribution of different energy levels to generate a broad UV/Vis absorption band and excitation-dependent emission spectra. Li et al. [25] conclude that the strong emission of CQDs comes from the quantum-sized structure itself instead of the carbon–oxygen surface. Xu et al. [26] considered the PL of CFNs may also be derived from the different surface oxidation degrees. According to our work, we believe that the origin PL behavior of the CFNs in this work may attribute to the different size of CFNs (Rand from 2 to 18nm) and different surface states from the oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the emission spectra of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film. Compared with Fig. 3b, a similar excitation-dependent behavior can be observed in the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film. However, as shown in Fig. 4b, the emission position shows obvious blue-shift in the emission spectrum of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film compared with that of the CFNs aqueous solution. This means that after the CFNs was hybridized with PVA to form thin film, the rigid and compact PVA matrix changed the energy level of the CFNs between the first excited state and the ground state, which is similar to the reported photoluminescence properties of Ru(II) complex within silica matrix [27].

Fig. 5 shows the afterglow intensity decay curve of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film. The afterglow emission decreases quickly at first and then slowly. This type of behavior is common in many reports, especially in rare earth ions doped inorganic afterglow phosphor [28,29]. The afterglow time which allowed being visually recognized (⩾0.32mcd/m2) is about 140s, suggesting potential application of this kind of the CFNs/PVA hybrid material in bioimaging field.

The afterglow emission spectrum and phosphorescence excitation spectrum of the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film are presented in Fig. 6 . The afterglow emission spectrum under 305nm excitation show a broad band ranging from 350nm to 590nm centering at 480nm, which shows about 100nm red shift compared with the photoluminescence emission spectrum of the CFNs/PVA film excited by the same wavelength. Therefore, the afterglow emission from the CFNs/PVA hybrid thin film was seen as green-light-emitting after the UV excitation light was turned off (see Fig. 5 inset, right). Using the phosphorescence measurement mode and monitoring at 480nm, two bands located at about 260nm and 305nm can be observed in the overlapped excitation region ranging from 205nm to 400nm. Combined with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum as shown in Fig. 3a, it is safe to conclude that because the aromatic carbonyl group has a close energy gap between the singlet and the triplet states, the spin–orbit coupling and intersystem crossing is efficient. Therefore, the afterglow of the CFNs/PVA film comes from the aromatic carbonyls in the CFNs.

Although a detailed mechanism is still not clear, a basic explanation can be obtained based on the above discussed results when origin of the afterglow is being concerned. When PVA is dissolved and the CFNs are dispersed in distilled water simultaneously, the interchain and intrachain hydrogen bonds of the PVA were broke up, giving opportunities to interactions between intrachain hydrogen bonds of the PVA and the surface groups of the CFNs. The subsequent drying and annealing of the PVA/CFNs solution leads to recrystallization of the PVA and “caging” the dispersed CFNs within the hydrogen bonded PVA–PVA matrix. Constraints from the matrix on the CFNs without quenching the emission may be the key of the special afterglow behavior. The PVA–PVA hydrogen bonding matrix also hinders motion of the CFNs, offering very rigid and compact dispersed CFNs in an extremely stable thin film. The dispersal also reduces the possibility of both exciton diffusion and interchain interaction [30]. Finally the PVA–PVA hydrogen bonding matrix changes the energy gap of the CFNs between the first excited singlet state and ground state, which may shift the energy of the first excited singlet state to be closer to the triplet of the CFNs and the intersystem crossing between singlet and triplet become more effectively. This could be the main reason that why the afterglow can be observed from the CFNs/PVA film, rather than from the CFNs aqueous solution, or from the CFNs/PVA solution.

Carbonaceous fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) were synthesized from silk cocoon via a simple thermal treatment procedure in inert atmosphere. The obtained CFNs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and as high as 23% quantum yield. Furthermore, obvious afterglow lasting for above 140 s can be seen by naked eyes after the CFNs were incorporated into a PVA matrix and were excited by UV light. Afterglow emission from the hybrid materials was analyzed to be ascribed to CO in the CFNs, and the PVA–PVA hydrogen bonding matrix plays an important role to the origin of the afterglow emission. The materials have great potential applications including chemical and biological sensing and time-resolved imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21171071, 21371062, 51372091), the project of the combination of Industry and Research by the Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2012B091100181), the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842), and the Science and Technology Innovating Project funded by the Guangdong Provincial Educational Commission of China (Grant No. 2013KJCX0030).
